topic: Two-zone disc topology — cross-equatorial coordinate improvement, NH regression
our_argument_summary: WIN-057 claims two-zone disc topology reduces cross-equatorial errors from 25–78% to 6.2% RMSE. Our verdict is Misleading: the improvement came from adding a fitted parameter (equatorial ring radius 14,105 km) to match six known WGS84 routes — curve-fitting, not prediction. Result remains vastly worse than standard geodesy and excludes equatorial cities entirely (OPEN-015). All major Cycle 1 rewrites were applied; the text is now substantially stronger.

relevant_files: monitor.py — no domain for WIN-057 or coordinate accuracy (confirmed: no change since Cycle 1)
Dome coordinates page — V13 two-zone topology, equatorial ring at 14,105 km
Dome model page — lists open problems including OPEN-001 (WGS84 dependence)
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: No monitoring infrastructure for WIN-057. Coordinate accuracy is a static geometric claim with tabulated route errors, not continuously validated. No change from Cycle 1.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: No monitoring exists. Static table of pre-computed V12 vs V13 route errors on dome's coordinates page.
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: OPEN-015 concedes near-equatorial cities 'fall beyond the equatorial ring.' Equatorial ring radius (14,105 km) is a free parameter that could be adjusted in future versions. Additionally, the EW angular scale (0.9941) and H(r) parameters could all be re-tuned, giving the model multiple degrees of freedom to absorb future discrepancies.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None. Our review makes no specific claims about dome monitoring code for this WIN, which is appropriate since no monitoring exists.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: Searched monitor.py — no domain exists for WIN-057, coordinate accuracy, two-zone topology, or distance validation. The monitored domains are geomagnetic, seismic, Schumann, and aviation-related. Coordinate accuracy is a static claim with no automated validation. Unchanged from Cycle 1.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Two-zone disc topology with equatorial ring at 14,105 km is a genuinely dome-specific geometric construct. Standard physics has no 'equatorial ring on a flat disc.' The dome is creating novel (incorrect) geometry to approximate known globe distances, not relabeling an existing standard physics explanation.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: All six cross-equatorial routes (Sydney-Buenos Aires, Sydney-Cape Town, etc.) have known distances published for decades via WGS84 geodesy. V13 was introduced in V51.0 (late March 2026) to fit these existing measurements. No distance was predicted before measurement and then confirmed.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: The two-zone formula structure (r_SH = 2×r_eq − r_NH) and equatorial ring concept are derived from dome disc geometry. However, the specific parameter value (14,105 km) was fitted to minimize residuals against known WGS84 distances — the framework is dome-derived but the calibration is observationally constrained. This creates a tension with our review text that says 'not derived from dome geometry,' which refers specifically to the parameter value, not the formula structure. Both characterizations are partially correct.

holes_found.description: Internal numerical inconsistency: 600× vs 60,000×. The finding and detail_verdict_text say the dome's result is '600× worse than standard geodesy (sub-0.01%),' while the detail_evidence says 'the dome's 6.2% RMSE... is over 60,000× worse' (comparing to 'better than 0.0001%'). These are a factor of 100 apart. Both denominators are defensible characterizations of Vincenty accuracy, but using two different values in the same review entry produces an internal contradiction. A dome defender would exploit this: 'Your review can't consistently state how bad our model is — 600× or 60,000×? Which is it? This sloppiness undermines your credibility.' The detail_extra also uses a third figure: 'sub-0.01%' matching the finding. So the detail_evidence is the outlier using the much more precise 0.0001% figure.
holes_found.recommendation: Pick one consistent comparison and use it throughout all four text fields. Options: (a) Use 'sub-0.01%' / '600×' everywhere — more conservative, harder to challenge. (b) Use 'better than 0.0001%' / '60,000×' everywhere — more dramatic, still defensible for intercontinental distances. Option (a) is safer. In detail_evidence, change 'better than 0.0001%' to 'better than 0.01%' and '60,000×' to '600×'. This aligns all fields.

holes_found.description: The 'not derived from dome geometry' characterization in detail_evidence creates tension with the code_analysis tag derives_from_dome: true. The text says the 14,105 km value is 'not derived from dome geometry' — meaning the specific numerical value was fitted, not geometrically derived. But our own tag says derives_from_dome is true because the two-zone formula framework IS dome-derived. A dome defender could use our own tags against our text: 'Even your own structural analysis admits the prediction derives from dome geometry.' The distinction between 'framework derived from dome geometry' and 'parameter fitted to observations' is valid but needs to be stated explicitly in the text so readers don't see a contradiction.
holes_found.recommendation: In detail_evidence, change 'not derived from dome geometry' to something more precise: 'a value not derived a priori from dome geometry but fitted post-hoc to minimize residuals against six known WGS84 distances.' Alternatively: 'a value whose magnitude was chosen to minimize known errors, not derived from any dome geometric principle.' This preserves the curve-fitting argument while acknowledging that the formula structure (though not the calibration) is dome-derived.

holes_found.description: OPEN-015 is mentioned three times: twice in detail_evidence (mid-paragraph and near the end) and again in detail_extra. The double mention in detail_evidence is particularly noticeable — same quote ('fall beyond the range') used 130 words apart. This redundancy weakens the prose by making it look padded. The first mention in detail_evidence is sufficient; the second is nearly identical. Detail_extra's elaboration is warranted since it adds specific city examples, but it overlaps heavily with the second detail_evidence mention.
holes_found.recommendation: Remove the second OPEN-015 mention in detail_evidence (the sentence starting 'The dome's own OPEN-001 concedes...' through 'the cherry-picked examples.'). Fold the OPEN-001 WGS84 dependence point into the first paragraph (it's a separate argument about input assumptions). Leave detail_extra's OPEN-015 analysis intact since it provides the deeper city-by-city breakdown.

holes_found.description: The '~40% of the world's population' claim for the ±12° equatorial band may be overstated. The tropics (±23.4°) contain roughly 40% of world population. The ±12° band is about half that latitude range and probably contains 15–25% of the world's population. The detail_extra then says 'approximately three billion people,' which at 37.5% of 8 billion reinforces the same potentially inflated figure. A dome defender could fact-check this and use the overstatement to question our rigor generally. The argument is strong even with a more conservative figure — excluding the equatorial band affects over a billion people regardless.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) verify the ±12° population figure with a proper source and cite it, (b) use a more conservative estimate ('roughly 20% of the world's population' / 'over one billion people'), or (c) replace with a qualitative statement: 'This exclusion affects major equatorial population centers including Singapore, Jakarta, Lagos, Nairobi, Bogotá, and Kuala Lumpur.' Option (c) is safest — listing specific cities is more powerful than a disputed percentage.

holes_found.description: The detail_evidence paragraph is very long (1,781 characters) and covers multiple distinct arguments in a single block. It discusses: parameter fitting, parameter count comparison, NH regression, Vincenty comparison, OPEN-015/OPEN-001 exclusion zones, and selection bias — all in one paragraph. This makes it harder to read and process. By contrast, detail_extra is well-structured with two clearly labeled subsections.
holes_found.recommendation: This is a style issue, not a factual one. The arguments are all correct. If the text is ever reformatted, breaking detail_evidence into shorter paragraphs or using HTML paragraph tags would improve readability.

stronger_arguments.description: A dome defender's best counter to the 'curve-fitting' argument: 'WGS84's own parameters (a = 6,378,137 m, f = 1/298.257) were also fitted to observations — satellite geodesy measurements. You criticize us for fitting 14,105 km to six routes, but all physical models fit parameters to data. The difference is quantitative, not qualitative.' This is a valid point that our review doesn't preempt. The rebuttal is: (1) WGS84 parameters were fitted to millions of satellite observations spanning the entire Earth, not six cherry-picked routes; (2) WGS84 achieves sub-millimeter accuracy globally with no exclusion zones, while the dome achieves 6.2% RMSE on six routes and fails entirely at the equator; (3) WGS84 parameters generate successful predictions across all geodetic applications (GPS, surveying, aviation), while the dome's parameters have never made a prospective distance prediction. Adding this preemptive rebuttal would strengthen the text.
sources: NGA/GPS.GOV documentation on WGS84 parameter determination

stronger_arguments.description: The dome's own v22 audit (preserved in evolution.txt) concluded: 'Southern arc distances 2x too long — FAILS. Globe wins cross-equator.' The author's own earlier version recognized the dome model fails on cross-equatorial distances. V13's 'fix' reduced the error but didn't resolve the fundamental geometric problem — a flat disc cannot reproduce great-circle distances on a sphere. Citing the dome's own v22 conclusion against WIN-057 would be powerful: 'The author's own v22 audit concluded the dome fails on cross-equatorial distances. V13 reduced but did not eliminate this failure.'

kernel_of_truth.description: The V13 iteration represents genuine scientific methodology in one narrow sense: the author identified a serious failure (25–78% cross-equatorial errors), introduced a structural modification (two-zone topology), and documented both the improvement (6.2% RMSE) and the regression (NH 5.2% → 7.3%). The honest acknowledgment of NH degradation is commendable. Most pseudoscientific models hide regressions; this one reported it. The iterative improvement from V9 through V12 to V13, with honest error tracking, is better methodology than most alternative cosmology efforts.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Improving a model's fit to existing data by adding parameters is retroactive curve-fitting, not prediction. No cross-equatorial distance was predicted before measurement and then confirmed. The 'confirmed' status means only that the dome's post-hoc fitted parameters reproduce known values — the definition of circular validation. Even after fitting, the dome's accuracy (6.2% RMSE) is vastly worse than standard geodesy, and the model breaks entirely at the equator (OPEN-015). Honest error reporting is good practice but doesn't validate the model when the errors remain enormous and the evidence base is six cherry-picked routes.

deeper_analysis:
Cycle 2 assessment: All five Cycle 1 recommendations were applied. The rewrite is substantially better — the strawman has been fixed, the finding now addresses the actual claim, OPEN-015 is covered in depth, and the Vincenty comparison is specific. The verdict (Misleading) is correct and well-supported.

The main remaining issue is the 600× vs 60,000× internal inconsistency, which is a straightforward editorial fix. The OPEN-015 redundancy and population figure are moderate issues that should be cleaned up but don't undermine the core argument.

The derives_from_dome tag tension is worth noting for the decider: our text says 'not derived from dome geometry' (referring to the parameter value) while the code_analysis tag says derives_from_dome: true (referring to the formula framework). Both are correct in their respective contexts, but the wording in the text should be more precise to avoid the appearance of self-contradiction.

One area our review could strengthen: preempting the 'all models fit parameters' counter-argument. The dome defender's strongest rebuttal to our curve-fitting critique is that WGS84 also uses observationally-constrained parameters. The difference is scale (millions of global observations vs. six routes) and result quality (sub-mm vs. 6.2% RMSE), but we should state this explicitly rather than leaving it as an unaddressed counter-argument.

The v22 self-critique is an underused argument — the dome's own earlier version concluded 'Globe wins cross-equator,' and V13 only partially walked that back.

Overall confidence in the current text is high. The verdict is correct, the core argument (curve-fitting, not prediction) is sound, and the evidence is well-presented. The issues found are editorial, not structural.

citations_verified: Vincenty (1975) DOI: 10.1179/sre.1975.23.176.88 — Confirmed valid in Cycle 1. T. Vincenty, 'Direct and Inverse Solutions of Geodesics on the Ellipsoid with Application of Nested Equations,' Survey Review, 23(176), 88-93, 1975.
citations_unchecked: NIMA TR8350.2 (WGS84 specification) — referenced implicitly via the WGS84 parameters a and f; not explicitly cited in our text but is the authoritative source for the values used.
OPEN-015 content — sourced from V51.0 dome coordinates page, not archived in raw-text/ (which still contains V50.6). Should be archived for long-term verifiability.
